title: Writing - Part 1: Social Media
description: Your class has been discussing the effects of social media on young people's lives.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the effects in your notes. You should explain which effect you think is more significant, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Social media makes people feel lonely even when connected.
It is the best way to keep in touch with distant friends.
Young people waste too much time scrolling.

structure.introduction: Introduce social media and the two effects you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first effect (e.g., staying in touch with friends) and its benefits.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second effect (e.g., comparing yourself to others) and its drawbacks.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both effects and state which you find more significant.

content.modelAnswer:
In recent years, social media has become an essential part of daily life for millions of young people worldwide. While it offers many advantages, it also raises important concerns. This essay will consider the effects of staying connected with friends and the habit of comparing oneself to others.

One of the main benefits of social media is its ability to help people maintain friendships over long distances. With a simple message or video call, individuals can easily keep in touch with family and friends who live far away. This can be especially valuable for those who have moved to a new city or country.

On the other hand, social media often encourages users to compare their lives to others. Seeing carefully edited photos of people's holidays, achievements, and lifestyles can make young people feel inadequate. Research suggests this habit is linked to lower self-esteem and increased anxiety among teenagers.

In conclusion, while social media is a powerful tool for staying connected, I believe its tendency to promote comparison is the more significant issue. Young people need guidance on how to use these platforms healthily and critically.
